problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy) on climate-driven migration: sea-level rise threatens to submerge low-lying island nations, creating "environmentally displaced persons" (EDPs/climate refugees) who fall outside existing refugee conventions because there is no human "persecutor." The team had to design a system that decides how many people must be relocated, when third-party (UN) intervention is triggered, which host countries should accept them, what rights/treatment they receive, how cultural loss is valued and preserved, and who pays. This paper (control number 2006291, titled "NOT ME, US!") built an end-to-end "Immigration Decision System" of coupled sub-models and validated it on two cases: a gradual case (Maldives, evacuation by ~2050) and a sudden-shock case (a catastrophic disaster in Japan).
models_used: Model I-1 — Sea-level/refugee forecasting: a time-series regression SL = c + b1*temp + b2*temp^2 + b3*t + mu, made stationary via first-order differencing (delta), fit against IPCC temperature/sea-level data; fitted form delta_SL = 8.826 - 2.086*delta_temp + 0.071*delta_temp^2 + 0.003*t

Geometric flooded-land model: approximates each territory as a flat circle, intrusion radius r = beta*SL (calibrated at 35cm rise per km of land), submerged area S_subm = pi*[R^2 - (R-r)^2]; affected population via a two-case piecewise formula keyed on whether erosion stays within the 150km offshore band (which holds ~50% of population)

Model I-2 — Culture Loss Evaluation: weighted sum V = w_h*heritage + w_l*sum(AVE_i*I_i) + w_c*lambda*GDP, decomposing cultural value into cultural heritage (World Heritage/World Bank valuations), natural landscape (tourism gross output discounted over a landscape lifetime), and customs/dialect (adjustment coefficient times GDP)

Model II-1 — Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP): 3-level hierarchy (goal / 3 criteria: Rights&Development, Natural Environment, Culture&Values / 8 sub-criteria B1-B8) to rank candidate host countries by natural+cultural match; consistency check via CR with reported lambda_max=3.007, CR=0.0068

Model II-2 — Immigration Stress Level (ISL): an original political-tolerance index built from standardized macro indicators (inflation, unemployment, crime rate, population growth, per-capita GDP growth), combined as positive/negative-correlation terms multiplied/divided by a squared native-population term ZNP^2 (marginal-utility / loss-aversion encoding), weighted-averaged with weights [2,1,2,0.5,1]; threshold ~12 calibrated empirically against Trump-2016, Italy and Hungary right-wing wins

Model II-3 — Nonlinear Programming: maximize welfare = sum(W_ij * mark_ij) subject to time limit t<=t0, stress_jt <= stress_0, conservation of EDP shares; solved in two steps (max yearly intake per country under the stress threshold, then allocate shares W_ij)

Model II-4 — Cost & Obligation: Total cost = N*cost_per + V_culture*cost_culptc; obligations apportioned by historical carbon emissions weighted by an 'encourage' incentive coefficient defined as the geometric mean of recent per-capita emission changes; applied to G20

Model III — Temporary Residence (sudden-shock): extreme-value normalized multi-criteria score C = w1*(1/L) + w2*(P_in/P_out) + w3*S over spatial distance, population carrying ratio, and host area with weights (0.4,0.3,0.3); a greedy descending-score absorption algorithm with a per-country cap (5%, scaled up for large populations) that re-distributes overflow

Supporting forecasting/uncertainty tools: Monte Carlo simulation to inject economic-crisis uncertainty into population/GDP/crime forecasts; Phillips curve and Okun's law to derive unemployment and inflation from GDP-growth forecasts
key_techniques: Decomposing an open-ended policy prompt into a pipeline of named sub-models (I-1, I-2, II-1..II-4, III) where each model's output feeds the next (forecast -> host ranking -> capacity -> schedule -> cost), giving a system rather than disconnected answers

Empirical calibration of an invented index against real political events: setting the ISL 'rejection threshold' (~12) by back-computing the stress index for the US (2016), Italy and Hungary at the moments right-wing/anti-immigration parties won

Embedding behavioral-economics theory (diminishing marginal utility of gains, amplified marginal aversion to loss) directly into a formula via the squared native-population term ZNP^2, and a recency-bias argument for using recent-relative (differenced) data
Geometric simplification: treating each country as a flat circle to turn a hard GIS/elevation problem into closed-form area and population formulas

Fairness/incentive design in cost allocation: weighting historical-emission responsibility by a geometric-mean 'encourage' factor so recent emission-cutters pay less, turning a cost-split into a policy incentive
Standardization discipline: projecting heterogeneous indicators to fixed intervals ([1,2], [0,4], [0,1]) before combining, with explicit positive/negative correlation handling
Two-case validation covering both regimes the prompt implies: a slow predictable case (Maldives) and a sudden-shock case (Japan) that activates the otherwise-idle Temporary Residence model
Scenario analysis within a case (Maldives Scenario I unconstrained time vs Scenario II forced 5-year evacuation) to show how the plan and stress levels respond to urgency
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins on systems thinking and policy realism, not on any single sophisticated algorithm. It answers every clause of an intentionally vague policy prompt (how many, when, where, what rights, what cultural cost, who pays) with a dedicated, interlocking model, and the outputs are concrete and decision-ready: migrate Maldivians to Malaysia starting 2044 (7 years before 2050), ~70-80k/year capped by an immigration-stress threshold, total cost ~$3.43B billed to the G20 by historical emissions. The standout differentiator is the Immigration Stress Level model: inventing a political-tolerance index and then calibrating its rejection threshold against actual elections (Trump, Italy's Five Star, Hungary) is exactly the kind of original, real-world-grounded modeling judges reward in a policy problem, and it directly addresses the prompt's 'personal choice / nation-state responsibility' framing. The carbon-emission cost allocation with an emission-reduction incentive shows ethical and policy nuance ('NOT ME, US!'). Two contrasting validation cases plus Monte Carlo uncertainty and an economic-crisis dip in the Maldives schedule signal robustness and self-criticism. The work reads as an actionable UN recommendation, which is the genre a Problem F judge is looking for."
section_highlights: "Opens with a one-page 'Our Recommendation' that splits guidance into the two operating regimes (gradual rise vs sudden tsunami) before any math — front-loading the policy answer for a skimming judge. A clear Problem Restatement flowchart (sea-level rise -> submerged habitat -> refugee -> resettlement -> treatment) frames scope. The body is rigorously sign-posted (Model I-1, I-2, II-1...III) so each model's role and hand-off is explicit; every sub-model states a guiding principle ('protect culture/human rights', 'minimize local impact') before its equations, tying math back to policy. Strong use of figures: AHP hierarchy diagram, ISL line chart of US/Italy/Hungary stress over 2008-2018 used to justify the threshold, obligation pie chart, and the Temporary-Residence algorithm flowchart. The Maldives case is concrete and quantitative (elevation 1.8m, sea level 0.67m by 2035 / 1.44m by 2050, cultural loss $22.46B, per-country G20 payment table). MATLAB source for the ISL index and raw US data tables are included in the Appendix, showing reproducibility."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The 'flat circle' country geometry is a severe physical oversimplification — for an archipelago like the Maldives (1,192 coral islands, mean elevation 1.8m), a single circular-disk erosion model has no real relationship to which atolls flood, yet it drives the headline population/timing numbers

The sea-level regression has internal inconsistencies and shaky reporting: the differenced equation drops the intercept logic, a constant 8.826 on a differenced series is suspicious, units are muddled, and a typo '20450' appears; the physical 35cm-rise-per-km calibration is asserted from a single secondary source

References are essentially broken: refs [1][2][4][5] are all the same Feng 2019 law paper, [3] and [6] are both the AVISO data URL, [7] is a Wikipedia coastline list — the empirical claims (population-coastline split, intrusion ratio, culture-weighting) are weakly sourced

Reproducibility/notation problems: several formulas have garbled symbols and OCR/mojibake (the Temporary Residence section and Chinese-comment MATLAB), B1/B2 score symbol collision, and the ISL piecewise projection constants (3.75/4, 4/7) are presented without derivation

Weights are largely hand-set rather than derived: ISL weights [2,1,2,0.5,1], temporary-residence weights (0.4,0.3,0.3) 'from literature', and AHP pairwise matrices are relegated to an appendix, so the ranking (Malaysia #1) is sensitive to subjective judgments not stress-tested

The ISL threshold calibration, while clever, generalizes a global '~12' threshold from only three Western countries and a single anti-immigration-vote event each — thin evidence, and political backlash to climate EDPs may differ from war-refugee backlash

Cost model is coarse: a flat $6,000/person 'national treatment' cost and a 5%-of-cultural-value protection budget are point assumptions with no sensitivity analysis; the Japan case table mixes population units (millions) with raw EDP counts and looks numerically inconsistent

No formal sensitivity or error analysis on the coupled pipeline — uncertainty enters only via Monte Carlo on a few macro inputs, so error propagation from the geometry and regression into the final cost/timing is unquantified
reusable_patterns: Sub-model pipeline architecture for open-ended policy prompts: forecast quantities -> rank options (AHP/MCDM) -> bound feasibility (a capacity/threshold model) -> schedule/optimize (NLP) -> allocate cost/responsibility. This skeleton transfers to almost any 'who, how many, when, where, who pays' policy problem

Invent a domain index, then calibrate its threshold against real-world events that 'crossed' it (here: anti-immigration election wins) — a repeatable way to give a custom metric an empirically defensible cutoff instead of an arbitrary number
Encode behavioral/economic theory as a functional form (squared term for loss aversion; differenced/recent-relative data for recency bias) to justify nonlinearity rather than fitting it blindly

Geometric-simplification trick: approximate a hard spatial/GIS quantity with a closed-form shape (circle/disk) to get tractable area-and-population formulas — fast, but flag it explicitly as a limitation

Fairness-with-incentive cost allocation: split a shared cost by historical responsibility but multiply by an incentive coefficient (geometric mean of recent improvement) so good recent behavior is rewarded — reusable for any 'polluter pays / shared burden' apportionment
Two-regime case study design: pick one nominal/gradual case and one stress/shock case so that every model (including the rarely-triggered emergency one) gets exercised and validated
Front-load an executive 'Our Recommendation' page that states the actionable policy answer per scenario before the modeling — strong structure for any judge skim
Standardize-before-combine discipline with explicit positive/negative-correlation handling when fusing heterogeneous indicators into one score (applies to ISL and the temporary-residence MCDM)
